source: IPSO ruling, Lancashire Telegraph school-attack story (single anonymous source); Editors' Code Clause 1 (Accuracy)
article: A teacher at Queen Elizabeth's Grammar School was attacked by a pupil wielding a knife in a classroom last week, leaving staff terrified, according to a parent who contacted this newspaper anonymously. The school has not commented.

critiques.text_quote: was attacked by a pupil wielding a knife in a classroom
critiques.question: This is a serious, specific allegation and the only thing holding it up is one anonymous parent who emailed us. We have not put it to the school, and 'has not commented' may just mean we did not ask properly. If the parent is wrong, or carrying a grudge, what is left?
critiques.why_it_matters: IPSO upheld a breach against the Lancashire Telegraph for almost exactly this: an attack story built on a single anonymous source, stated as established fact, not properly put to the school before publication.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Get a second source, a staff member, another parent, a police log, or attribute the whole account to the parent and label it unverified. Then put the specific allegation to the school with real time to respond, and say in the piece what we asked and when.
critiques.replacement: was attacked by a pupil, according to a parent who contacted this newspaper, an account the school has not yet confirmed

source: IPSO rulings on anonymous single-source claims presented as fact; Editors' Code Clause 1
article: The council's finance director took payments from a contractor in exchange for steering a 2 million pound waste contract, sources said.

critiques.text_quote: took payments from a contractor in exchange for steering a 2 million pound waste contract
critiques.question: 'Took payments in exchange for steering a contract' describes bribery, a crime. We state it as fact and hang it on 'sources' with nothing else visible. Do we have the payment records, a named second source, an audit?
critiques.why_it_matters: IPSO has repeatedly ruled that presenting an anonymous source's claim as fact rather than clearly as a claim breaches the accuracy clause. For a criminal allegation against a named official it is also the core fact pattern of a libel suit.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Attribute it plainly and soften the verb until we can prove intent. Keep whatever documentary evidence we have in the file in case we have to defend it.
critiques.replacement: is alleged by sources to have accepted payments from a contractor that later won a 2 million pound waste contract

source: Editors' Code Clause 1 (Accuracy); standard pre-publication defamation review
article: City councilman Mark Reyes stole more than 400,000 dollars from a youth sports nonprofit he chaired, according to a former board member.

critiques.question: 'Stole' is theft, a specific crime with specific elements. Do we have an indictment, a civil judgment, or an audit? A former board member's account, even an honest one, is not a finding.
critiques.why_it_matters: Stating as fact that a named person committed a crime, with no charge and one source, is textbook defamation per se. The verb is the first thing opposing counsel circles.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Soften the verb to what we can defend and attribute it. If we hold the audit, anchor the number to the audit instead of pinning the verb on the person.
critiques.replacement: is alleged to have diverted more than 400,000 dollars

source: Editors' Code Clause 1; standard pre-publication defamation review
article: Documents obtained by the Herald show hospital administrator Susan Kemp defrauded Medicare through a billing scheme that prosecutors are now investigating.

critiques.text_quote: defrauded Medicare through a billing scheme
critiques.question: The same sentence says prosecutors are still investigating, which means no charge has been filed. But we have already used 'defrauded', the conclusion a court has not reached. Which is it?
critiques.why_it_matters: Stating that a named person committed fraud during an open investigation, before any charge, is one of the most common ways newsrooms get sued and lose. An investigation is not a finding.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Report the investigation, not the verdict. Save 'defrauded' for after an indictment or judgment, and attribute it to that document when it lands.
critiques.replacement: is under federal investigation over a Medicare billing scheme

source: Editors' Code Clause 1 and right-of-reply practice
article: The investigation found that Detective James Cole pressured at least three witnesses to change their statements. Cole could not be reached for comment.

critiques.text_quote: Cole could not be reached for comment
critiques.issue_label: thin right of reply on a serious allegation
critiques.question: We are accusing a named former officer of witness tampering, and his entire right of reply is five words. What did we actually try, and over how long?
critiques.why_it_matters: The Editors' Code requires a fair opportunity to reply to significant allegations. 'Could not be reached', with no detail, is the line that makes a regulator or a court conclude we did the minimum.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Document the outreach inside the article: the channels tried and the time window. If we genuinely could not reach him, say exactly what we did so the effort is on the record.
critiques.replacement: Cole did not respond to messages left with his lawyer and at two phone numbers over eight days

source: Editors' Code Clause 1; defamation review on opinion stated as fact
article: Internal emails show the chief executive ordered staff to delete the test records, a clear cover-up of the safety failures.

critiques.text_quote: a clear cover-up of the safety failures
critiques.question: 'A clear cover-up of the safety failures' is a conclusion, not something the emails say. No regulator has found a cover-up. We have. Are we ready to defend that as our own assertion?
critiques.why_it_matters: Calling a named executive's conduct a cover-up in our own voice turns a description into a legal conclusion we then have to prove. Attributing it, or describing the conduct plainly, keeps the protection.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Describe what the emails actually show and let the reader draw the inference, or attribute the word 'cover-up' to a named source who will stand behind it.
critiques.replacement: which former staff describe as an attempt to bury the safety failures

source: Editors' Code Clause 1; defamation review on paraphrase escalation
article: The whistleblower said the agency 'wasn't careful' with the figures. In other words, the agency falsified its budget reports.

critiques.text_quote: In other words, the agency falsified its budget reports
critiques.question: The source said 'wasn't careful'. We have translated that into 'falsified', which is a deliberate act and a far stronger, far riskier claim. Did the source actually say falsification, or did we upgrade it?
critiques.why_it_matters: Turning a hedged claim into a definite accusation is a libel exposure we manufactured ourselves. If sued, we cannot fall back on the source, because the source never said it.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Quote the source's actual words and let them stand, or go back and get the source to say on the record whether they mean deliberate falsification.
critiques.replacement: The whistleblower said the agency 'wasn't careful' with the figures

source: IPSO guidance on anonymous sourcing; defamation review on source independence
article: According to two people with knowledge of the matter, the director knew about the defect, hid it from regulators, and lied to the board.

critiques.text_quote: the director knew about the defect, hid it from regulators, and lied to the board
critiques.issue_label: one sourcing line carrying three allegations
critiques.question: Three separate and serious claims are all riding on the same 'two people with knowledge'. Is each claim sourced to both people, or is each person good for one piece of it? If those two turn out to be the same circle, the whole paragraph is single-sourced.
critiques.why_it_matters: Stacking multiple distinct allegations behind one anonymous attribution means one recantation collapses everything. For libel purposes, two sources from the same room are close to one source.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Separate the claims and show what supports each one. Keep the best-sourced allegation now, and hold the others until they are independently confirmed.

source: Editors' Code Clause 1; example of a properly sourced and balanced passage
article: Senator Linda Hart voted against the housing bill on Tuesday after receiving more than 200,000 dollars in donations from the real estate lobby over two years, federal disclosure filings show. Hart's office said the vote reflected concerns about zoning preemption.

source: Editors' Code Clause 1 distinction between comment and fact; fair comment
article: In my view, the mayor's housing plan is a giveaway to developers dressed up as reform, and voters should see it for what it is. This is an opinion column.

source: Editors' Code Clause 1; example of an on-the-record public figure within their role
article: The mayor told reporters at City Hall on Wednesday that she would veto the council's budget, calling it 'fiscally reckless.'
